stimulus: “Where the Streetlamps Meet the Pines” is a 1934 poem by Aleksy Tomaszewski, who immigrated to Chicago as a young man from a village in the Tatra foothills of Poland. The poem conveys Tomaszewski’s contrasting feelings about Chicago—his adopted home in the United States—and his home country: ______blank
stem: Which quotation from “Where the Streetlamps Meet the Pines” most effectively illustrates the claim?

answerOptions.content: “A weary trolley clatters by, / Its bell a thin, late note; / Pale neon writes against the sky, / And cold wires float.”
“Dim figures start for the mill; / I watch them shuffle through the gloam, / ’Tis dawn, dawn in Chicago. / But I would be among the pines once more, / Among the pines that ring my mother’s door.”
“And the spotted goat is calling, calling, calling / From her trampled corner of the upland lea / That slopes down to the cold spring water falling / Over the smooth stones lazily!”
“The tired vans go grumbling by, / The moaning, groaning vans, / And the old fish carts go rattling by / Under the same dull lamps.”

rationale: Choice B is the best answer because the quotation contrasts a negative view of Chicago at dawn (workers shuffling through the gloom) with a positive longing for the speaker’s home country (“among the pines that ring my mother’s door”), directly illustrating the speaker’s contrasting feelings about the two places. Choice A is incorrect because the quotation describes only a Chicago scene. Choice C is incorrect because the quotation describes only the rural homeland without contrasting it to Chicago. Choice D is incorrect because the quotation focuses only on the negative urban setting and does not include the contrasting homeland.
